segments.title: Modern warships as cruisers by function
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke argues that many modern surface combatants perform the traditional cruising role, while labels such as destroyer and frigate often describe wartime functions.
segments.body: Looking out from Mikasa, Dr. Clarke reflects on how modern warships should be understood in both form and function. He argues that their everyday roles are often closer to the traditional cruiser: ships that range widely, show presence, and undertake varied duties in peacetime. By contrast, names such as destroyer and frigate can emphasize particular wartime functions. The distinction invites viewers to look beyond a hull's label and ask what it is expected to do across its service life. It is a useful reminder that naval classification changes as fleet roles change.

evidence.note: He describes modern ships as cruisers in the traditional peacetime-role sense and contrasts that with destroyer and frigate labels.

segments.title: Uncrewed assets around crewed task-group centres
segments.summary: He imagines future task groups centred on crewed warships, with uncrewed vessels extending the group's outer edges and capabilities.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expects uncrewed vessels to become part of task-group architecture rather than simply isolated tools. In his sketch, crewed ships remain the centres around which uncrewed assets operate. Those assets can form the outer edges of a group and contribute capabilities without displacing the human-commanded warship at its core. He extends the idea to task forces made up of multiple such groups and their flotillas. The comparison places current uncrewed-system debates within a longer naval pattern of organising ships around a principal command and support structure.

evidence.note: He outlines crewed ships at the centre of groups with uncrewed assets around their edges and argues their wartime function needs recognition.

segments.title: Why “sloop” fits flexible uncrewed vessels
segments.summary: Using Mikasa as a historical setting, Dr. Clarke proposes “sloop” as a useful name for adaptable uncrewed vessels that carry out whatever larger ships need done.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke turns to the historical idea of the sloop to describe a possible future role for uncrewed vessels. In the era he has in mind, a sloop was a flexible ship expected to go where needed and undertake tasks that larger vessels required. The defining idea was not a narrow mission but adaptability. He therefore suggests that the term captures the likely utility of uncrewed assets better than a purely technical label. The proposal is an analogy, not a claim that future systems will duplicate historic sloops, but it gives fleet planners a functional vocabulary for thinking about them.

evidence.note: He defines the historical sloop as a flexible vessel that performed what larger ships required and applies that analogy to uncrewed assets.
